State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 907 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660         322-5660              322-5901              322-6441              322-6444

February 3, 1981

Thomas W. Oglesby
City Manager
City of Camarillo
601 Carmen Drive
P.O. Box 248
Camarillo, CA 93010

Dear Mr. Oglesby:

In your letter to us of December 30, 1980, as well as
our later phone conversation, you asked whether the City of
Camarillo was required by the Political Reform Act to adopt a
conflict of interest code for its newly established Rent
Review Board. The facts as I understand them are as follows.

The membership of the Rent Review Board is comprised of
unpaid volunteers from the community including both landlords
and tenants. The function of the Board is to investigate and
mediate landlord-tenant disputes for the purpose of assisting
in their voluntary settlement. Although the Board has the
authority to hold hearings it has no subpoena power so that
attendance at such hearings is strictly voluntary. The Board's
recommendation as to settlement of any dispute is purely
advisory and not appealable or binding on the parties. The
estimated budget of the Board is under $1,000 per year.

Under Section 87300 every government agency is required
to adopt and promulgate a conflict of interest code. The
Fair Political Practices Commission has approved the issuance
of exemptions from this requirement if the agency in question
would have no "designated employees." Section 82019 defines
a "designated employee: as one whose position

entails the making or participation in the making of
decisions which may have a material effect on any financial
interest. [It] does not include ... any unsalaried
member of any board or commission which serves a solely
advisory function.

Thomas W. Oglesby
February 3, 1981
Page Two


     From the facts you have given me it appears that the
Board members perform a solely advisory function and cannot
foreseeably use their official positions to influence any
governmental decisions. Assuming that this is the situation,
there would be no "designated employees" and thus, no requirement that the Rent Review Board adopt a conflict of interest
code.

     It should be noted that a board will not be characterized
as "solely advisory" whenever

     It makes substantive recommendations which are, and over
an extended period of time have been regularly approved
without significant amendment or modification by another
public official or governmental agency.

                                    2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
                                    18700(a)(1)(c).

     Thus, if it appears in the future that courts of law or other
government agencies are giving weight to recommendations of
the Rent Review Board when making decisions then it will
become necessary for the Board to adopt a Code.

     In conclusion, the Rent Review Board established by the
City of Camarillo is exempt from the requirement of adopting
a conflict of interest code because it possesses no decision-
making authority but performs a solely advisory function.
Please call me if I can be of further help.

                                    Sincerely,


                                    Stella Connell Levy
                                    Counsel
                                    Legal Division

CITY OF CAMARILLO
601 CARMEN DRIVE
P.O. BOX 240
CAMARILLO, CALIFORNIA 93010
(805) 482-8921

OFFICE OF THE CITY MANAGER

January 5, 1981


Mr. Michael Bennett, Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street Building
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Bennett:

In my letter of December 30, 1980 relative to the Camarillo Rent
Review Board, we inadvertently enclosed the wrong resolution. Resolution No. 80-263 is the proper document to be considered in reviewing our request.

Sincerely,

[signature]
THOMAS W. OGLESBY

TWO:1c
Enclosure

CITY OF CAMARILLO
601 CARMEN DRIVE
P.O. BOX 248
CAMARILLO, CALIFORNIA 93010
(805) 482-8021

OFFICE OF THE CITY MANAGER

December 30, 1980


Mr. Michael Bennett, Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street Building
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Sir:

The City of Camarillo has established a Rent Review Board (resolution enclosed),
the purpose of which is to investigate tenant/landlord disputes with the objective of arriving at a voluntary solution acceptable to all concerned parties.
The Board which has po enforcement authority consists of landlords and tenants
from rental housing projects within the community and other community representatives. The Board Members are recognized as unpaid City officials for the
purpose of providing City legal protection and liability exposure which
might result from the performance of their duties.

It is our belief that the Members of the Board are exempt from the financial
disclosure requirements, since their recommendations and decisions are advisory
only to the parties in dispute and are not subject to appeal and review by any
other authority. Our City Attorney shares in that belief, but has suggested
that we request a more definitive opinion from you in order that we may properly
advise the Board Members on this important matter.

We would appreciate your early review of this matter. If more information is
needed, please let me know.

Sincerely,

THOMAS W. OGLESBY

TWO:jc
Enclosure

cc: City Attorney Sorensen
    Board Chairman Meredth

RESOLUTION NO. SO-183

A RESOLUTION OF THE CITY COUNCIL
OF THE CITY OF CAMARILLO RECOGNIZING
AND APPROVING THE FORMATION OF A
RENT REVIEW COMMISSION

WHEREAS, pursuant to studies and hearings conducted by
the City Council and City Staff, it has been determined that rents
are rising rapidly and causing concern among a substantial number
of Camarillo tenants occupying mobile-homes and multiple family
residential projects in the city; and

WHEREAS, the Council finds that it is in the public
interest to assist the occupants of rental units from
unreasonable rent increases, while at the same time recognizing
Landlord's needs generally to have rental increases sufficient
to cover increased costs of operation and maintenance of rental
properties and to encourage improvement thereto; and

WHEREAS, based upon the foregoing the City Council
believes that the establishment of a voluntary community based
Rent Review Commission will provide an atmosphere conducive to
open communications between landlords and tenants and be the
basis for assisting in resolving rent dispute problems for both
landlords and tenants in the city.

NOW THEREFORE, the City Council of the city of Camarillo
does resolve as follows:

Section 1. Commission: The City Council recognizes
supports and approves the establishment of a Rent Review Commission
which shall act in an advisory capacity to assist in and make
recommendations concerning the solutions of landlord/tenant disputes
with the objective of producing an amicable, voluntary solution
to the dispute.

Section 2. Membership: The Rent Review Commission
shall consist of nine (9) members all of whom shall be appointed
by the City Council and all of whom shall serve at the pleasure
of the City Council without any fixed term or duration. Any or
all of said Commission members may be removed by the City Council
at its discretion without cause. Three of said members shall be
representatives of tenants of multiple family projects of five
units or more and tenants of mobile-home parks in the City, three
shall be representatives of owners of multiple family projects
of 5 units or more and mobile-home parks in the City, and three
members shall be persons who are not tenant or owner representatives
as described herein.

Section 3. Jurisdiction: The Commission, shall take
all necessary actions including hearings if necessary, to
assist in the solution and/or settlement of rental disputes
between tenants and landlords only in connection with mobile-home
park and multiple family resident projects of five (5) or more
units in the City. The Commission's authority to assist and
hold hearings if necessary on rental disputes concerning mobile-
home park tenants and owners, shall be limited to those disputes
brought before the Commission on petition of more than 25% of the
tenants in any one mobile-home park. No petition shall be
required in connection with rental disputes concerning tenants and
owners of family residential projects of 5 or more units in the
City. The Commission shall establish its own rules and regulations
with respect to the solution and/or settlement of disputes brought
before it including but not limited to rules regarding Commission
procedure and hearing procedure.

Section 4. Commission Action: the Commission's
recommendation, solution, and/or settlement of any dispute shall be

- 2 -

by a majority vote of its members and shall only be advisory
to tenants and landlords, and shall not be appealable or subject
to any review by this City Council.

Section 5. Expenses: The City will assume the cost of
all the usual and reasonable expenses of the Commission such as
stationary, postage and telephone costs related to the work of
the Commission other than those expenses directly attributable
to the holding of rent dispute hearings. The cost of recording
and/or transcription of any hearing before the Commission shall
be divided equally between the petitioning tenants and owners. The
Commission may require an appropriate deposit of costs in the
event the settlement and/or resolution of any rent dispute will
result in the Commission incurring costs and expenses not re-
imbursed by the City as provided herein.

Section 6. Miscellaneous: It is neither the intent
or desire of the City Council that the Commission established
hereunder be or have the status of a City Commission and therefore
the members of the Commission shall not be or are intended to
be officials, officers or employees of the City and the actions
of the Commission are not intended to represent any position or
policy of the City as to any rental dispute considered by the
Commission.

Section 7. Commission Report: Every six months the
Commission shall submit to the City Council for information
purposes only, a status report of the activities of the Commission
to date.

Section 8. The City Clerk shall certify to the adoption
of this resolution.

CA-195

APPROVED AND ADOPTED, this 27th day of August ,1980.

ATTEST:

[signature]
Mayor

[signature]
DeputyCity Clerk

STATE OF CALIFORNIA
COUNTY OF VENTURA
CITY OF CAMARILLO

I HEREBY CERTIFY, that the foregoing Resolution No. 80-183 was
approved and adopted by the City Council of the City of Camarillo
at a regular meeting held on the 27th day of August, 1980 by
the following vote, to wit:

AYES: Councilmen: Morgan, Eacy; Mayor Cayle

NOES: Councilmen: McConville

ABSENT: Councilmen: Moore

[signature]
DeputyCity Clerk

RESOLUTION NO. 80-263

A RESOLUTION OF THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY
OF CAMARILLO ESTABLISHING A RENT REVIEW COM-
MISSION AND RESCINDING RESOLUTION NO. 80-183

WHEREAS, pursuant to studies and hearings conducted by
the City Council and City Staff, it has been determined that
rents are rising rapidly and causing concern among a sub-
stantial number of Camarillo tenants occupying mobile-homes
and multiple family residential projects in the City; and

WHEREAS, the Council finds that it is in the public
interest to assist the occupants of rental units from un-
reasonable rent increases, while at the same time recognizing
Landlord's needs generally to have rental increases sufficient
to cover increased costs of operation and maintenance of rental
properties and to encourage improvements thereto; and

WHEREAS, based upon the foregoing the City Council believes
that the establishment of a voluntary community based Rent
Review Commission will provide an atmosphere conducive to open
communications between landlords and tenants and be the basis
for assisting in resolving rent dispute problems for both land-
lords and tenants in the City.

NOW, THEREFORE, the City Council of the City of Camarillo
does resolve as follows:

Section 1. Commission: The City Council hereby establishes
a Rent Review Commission which shall act in an advisory capacity
to assist in and make recommendations concerning the solutions of
landlord/tenant disputes with the objective of producing an
amicable, voluntary solution to the dispute.

Section 2. Membership: The Rent Review Commission shall
consist of nine (9) members all of whom shall be appointed by
the City Council and all of whom shall serve at the pleasure
of the City Council without any fixed term or duration. Any or
all of said Commission members may be removed by the City Council
at its discretion without cause. Three of said members shall be
representatives of tenants of multiple family projects of five
units or more and tenants of mobile-home parks in the City, three
shall be representatives of owners of multiple family projects
of 5 units or more and mobile-home parks in the City, and three
members shall be persons who are not tenant or owner representatives
as described herein.

Section 3. Jurisdiction: The Commission, shall take all
necessary actions including hearings if necessary, to assist in the
solution and/or settlement of rental disputes between tenants and
landlords only in connection with mobile-home park and multiple
family resident projects of five (5) or more units in the City.
The Commission's authority to conduct hearings and/or hold hearings
on rental disputes concerning mobile-home park tenants and owners,
shall be limited to those disputes brought before the Commission
on and/or petition of no more than 25% of the tenants in any one mobile-home
project. No petition shall be required in connection with rental dis-
putes concerning tenants and owners of family residential pro-
jects of 5 or more units in the City. The Commission shall estab-
lish its own rules and regulations with respect to the solution and/
or settlement of disputes brought before it including but not limited
to rules regarding Commission procedure and hearing procedure.

Section 4. Commission Action: the Commission's recommendation,
solution, and/or settlement of any dispute shall be by a majority
vote of its members and shall only be advisory to tenants and
landlords, and shall not be appealable or subject to any review
by this City Council.

Section 5. Expenses: The City will assume the cost of
all the usual and reasonable expenses of the Commission such as
stationery, postage and telephone costs related to the work of
the Commission other than those expenses directly attributable
to holding of rent dispute hearings. The cost of recording
and/or transcription of any hearing before the Commission shall
be divided equally between the petitioning tenants and owners.
The Commission may require an appropriate deposit or costs in
the event the settlement and/or resolution of any rent dispute
will result in the Commission incurring costs and expenses not
reimbursed by the City as provided herein.

Section 6. Commission Report: Every six months the
Commission shall submit to the City Council for information
purposes only, a status report of the activities of the Commission
to date.

Section 7. The City Clerk shall certify to the adoption of
this resolution.

Section 8. Resolution No. 80-183 hereby is rescinded.

APPROVED AND ADOPTED, this 17th day of December, 1980.

ATTEST:

[illegible]                          [illegible]
City Clerk                           Mayor

STATE OF CALIFORNIA  )
COUNTY OF VENTURA    ) ss.
CITY OF CAMARILLO    )

I HEREBY CERTIFY, that the foregoing Resolution No. 80-263
was approved and adopted by the City Council of the City of
Camarillo at a regular meeting held on the 17th day of December,
1980 by the following vote, to wit:

AYES: Councilmen: Morgan, Esty, Moore, Mayor Gayle

NOES: Councilmen: McConville

ABSENT: Councilmen: None


[illegible]
City Clerk

Although the Board has the authority to hold hearings it has no subpoena power so that attendance at such hearings is strictly voluntary. The Fair Political Practices Commission has approved the issuance of exemptions from this requirement if the agency in question would have no "designated employees."
